items.correct: Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs — often due to long-term economic change like deindustrialisation.
items.misconception: Structural unemployment is unemployment caused by a recession.
items.random_wrong: Structural unemployment is when people are too lazy to work.
items.why: Recession-driven unemployment is CYCLICAL. Structural is about long-term mismatches — coal miners can't easily become app developers. Students collapse all unemployment into 'lack of demand'.

items.q: What determines the size of the fiscal multiplier?
items.correct: Mainly the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — the higher the share of extra income people spend domestically, the bigger the multiplier. Leakages (saving, imports, taxes) reduce the multiplier.
items.misconception: The fiscal multiplier is always 2 or 3.
items.random_wrong: The fiscal multiplier is the rate of growth of the money supply.
items.why: The multiplier varies with conditions — bigger in slumps with high MPC and slack capacity, smaller at full employment or in open economies. Students learn a fixed number rather than the determining mechanism.

items.q: What components make up aggregate demand?
items.correct: AD = C + I + G + (X - M): consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports. A shift in any component shifts the AD curve.
items.misconception: Aggregate demand is just the total amount consumers want to buy.
items.random_wrong: Aggregate demand is the same as GDP measured by demand.
items.why: AD covers all spending sources, not just households. Students reduce it to consumer demand, missing that investment, government and trade are equally constitutive. Mis-attributing a shift then misdiagnoses the cause.

items.q: Ricardo published 'Principles of Political Economy and Taxation' in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1817. “1776” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's 1995. “1947” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 1817 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
